Students who have not completed the program in the 4 years are not considered.

DT feature importances in order to identinfy which courses aer the most important towards student success.

Unspecified rebalancing techniques are implemented.

Naive Bayes
ANN
RF-GI
RF-IG
RF-Acc

While the authors attempt to use multilpe classifiers, they are pretty dead set on using decision trees due to their interpretability.

Precision and recall are shown for the purposes of explaining confusion matrix results. However, these metrics are not factored in the decision for a model. 

Models struggle with identification of low represented classes.

DT feature selection highlights 5 courses as the most important: HS205/206, MS-121, CS-251, HS-207 and CT-255.

When it comes to pathways, the authors identify 2 rules conducive for bad performance and 1 rule for good performance.

Small sample size. 

Unclear motives for attempting to use algorithms outside simple DT based models.

tables.heading: Summary Table PRED
rows:
106 students for training data
104 students for test data
For clustering, each each student is represented by 4 vectors, each vector containing the grades for each academic year.

Each of the clusters refers to a progression sequence throughout their program.

Yearly Clusters are foer cohort 1 show the same patterns emerging: 

Students with Low performance tend to continue having low performance, students with Intermediate Performance tend to cotinue having intermediate performance and finally students with high performance tend to continue having high performance.

The results allowed for the development of a set of Pragmatic Policies usable to indentifyy students who may be at risk.

The non-inclusion of admission marks hinders the ability of putting into motion corrective measures right from the get go.

Pathway is defined by a element tuple comprising the cluster labels for each academic year. 

2 main groups emerge: good students on first year who continue being good throughout and bad students who continue to be bad throughout.

Clustering of progression does not consider pre admission data.

Some clusters have Sample Size of 1.

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: This work is divided into 2 different parts. The first is the prediction of undergraduate student performance at the end of a 4-year program. The second is the grouping of students, out of which 2 groups emerge: low and high achieving students.

text.content: This work is divided into 2 different parts. The first is the prediction of undergraduate student performance at the end of a 4-year program. The second is the grouping of students, out of which 2 groups emerge: low and high achieving students.

blocks.text: Authors also found that focusing the prediction on a smaller subset of courses may be a good early indicator of student performance. Student performance to be understood, in this context, as success at the end of a program.

blocks.text: Educational Data Mining seeks to make use of the enormous amounts of data that exist nowadays in universities and classification is among the most common tasks in EDM. Moreover, these models can work at different levels:

blocks.text: These models have been applied in the context of either one or more than one educational institutions. Different algorithms have been used: Decision Trees, Bayesian Networks, kNN. blocks.text: General metrics used is accuracy, which tends achieve better results in the binary classification case. Still, the use of early grades is essencial in order to be able to obtain what can be considered good predictions. A second consideration is that many works use cross-validation to assess the performance of their models. The authors will train the model on one co-hort and evaluate the performance of the model on another cohort.

blocks.text: 3. Data Mining Methods

text.content: 3. Data Mining Methods

rich_text.plain_text: 3. Data Mining Methods

blocks.text: The authors review some of the data mining methods they intend to emply in this work: classification and clustering. The metrics they identify as relevant for classification are Accuracy and the kappa coefficient (an indicator that takes into account that correct predictions could occur by chance). There is also an important distinction between models that are black box: ANN, NB, RF from the ones that are more interpretable such as Decision Trees.

text.content: The authors review some of the data mining methods they intend to emply in this work: classification and clustering. blocks.text: Clustering in this paper is defines as a process of grouping similar objects. Similar objects are usually defined as being closest using a certain metric, which in many situations is the euclidean distance. Common algorithms include k-means and the one used in the wok, which is going to be x-means. Both are centroid-based algorithms with a key distinction being that x-means attempts to find the optimal number of clusters using a Bayesian Information Criterion.

blocks.text: Methods

text.content: Methods

rich_text.plain_text: Methods

blocks.text:
The data uses 4-year information technology BSc degree in Pakistan, more specifically data 210 undergraduate students in the years 2007-2008 and 2008-2009. These wil go through a process of feature selection to select the most important marks to use. 

Target variable is the marks at the end of the program: 10% of first year average exams, 20% for second year, 30% for 3rd year and 40% of 4th year. Student who failed after the 4 years are not considered in the study, with the remaining 5 grade classes (A, B, C, D and E) being the relevant classification targets.

text.content:
The data uses 4-year information technology BSc degree in Pakistan, more specifically data 210 undergraduate students in the years 2007-2008 and 2008-2009. These wil go through a process of feature selection to select the most important marks to use. 

Target variable is the marks at the end of the program: 10% of first year average exams, 20% for second year, 30% for 3rd year and 40% of 4th year. Student who failed after the 4 years are not considered in the study, with the remaining 5 grade classes (A, B, C, D and E) being the relevant classification targets.

blocks.text: 1. Research Question 1

text.content: 1. Research Question 1

blocks.text: The authors employed multiple classification algorithms. From the analysis osf the sample, there are barely any A students while C is the most popular class. Majority class predictions would achieve 51% accuracy. More specifically, the authors look at the grade distribution to identify ideal intervals for prediction: ending up being grades B to E. Features considered end up being pre-admission marks and marks on years 1 and 2.

blocks.text: 2. Research Question 2

blocks.text: To address RQ2, the authors look into identifying which factors between courses from the first and second year that are predictive of student success. For that they explore the different feature selection techniques that are available in RapidMiner. Most did not improve performance of DTs (because DTs make it themselves). So, the authors turn to looking at the feature importances of the DT.

blocks.text: Variables that appear in at least 50% of the created decision trees are considered important. Then, the authors focus on decision paths that lead to successful outcomes (A and B) or unsuccessful outcomes (D and E).

blocks.text: Research Question 3

text.content: Research Question 3

rich_text.plain_text: Research Question 3

blocks.text: To study the progression paths, the authors employ clustering on multiple levels. For each year, they create vectors containing the marks obtained in each year. For each year, students are clustered using the Xmeans algorithm. Progression is then defined as a 4 element tuple containing the cluster labels for each year.

blocks.text: Results

text.content: Results

rich_text.plain_text: Results

blocks.text: 1. Prediction of student performance

text.content: 1. Prediction of student performance

rich_text.plain_text: 1. Prediction of student performance

blocks.text: The results on 10 different classifiers show that it is possible to predict student performance by training the models on one cohort of students and applying it to a second cohort. Interestingly, the model that achieves the best score is a Naive Bayes classification algorithm (84% accuracy and 0.73 kappa coefficient. By looking the confusion matrices, it is possible to see that only Decision Tree based algorithms are able to predict students who achieved an E.

text.content: The results on 10 different classifiers show that it is possible to predict student performance by training the models on one cohort of students and applying it to a second cohort. By looking the confusion matrices, it is possible to see that only Decision Tree based algorithms are able to predict students who achieved an E.

text.content: 2. Feature Importances

blocks.text: The authors rely on Decision Tree interpretability to determine which courses are the most important towards determining the final grade of the program. More particularly, they found 2 courses from first year and 3 courses from the second year to be the ones with the best predictive potential (HS205/206, MS-121, CS-251, HS-207 and CT-255).

blocks.text: The paper proceeds with a discussion of a would be pragmatic policy of using the pragmatic policy of using the marks of the first 2 years to identify students as “likely to have A or B” or “likely to be D and E” which intends to catch, as early as possible, both high performing students and students who are struggling. For that, they analyze the progressions of the students. Results show that applying this pragmatic policy would is cautious: when in doubt, flag students in need.
